channel.title: +Mathis on Vishvas's notes
channel.description: Recent content in +Mathis on Vishvas's notes

item.title: Impossible transition
item.description: Source: TW The impossible transition? The fatality of coal in the United Kingdom -A A +A Charles-François Mathis Université Bordeaux Montaigne, CEMMC, EA 2958, F-33607 Pessac, France charles-francois.mathis[at]u-bordeaux-montaigne.fr Abstract This article seeks to understand the reasons for Victorian fatalism towards coal dependency – which led to in an inability to abandon this energy – in an effort to better understand what an energy transition would actually entail. The article depicts a providential form of thought in relation to energy at the time, when coal was seen as being a God-given gift that led to world domination: Victorian society was subsequently guilt-ridden due to its waste of coal.

item.title: King coal rules
item.description: Source: TW Abstract| Index| Outline| Text| Bibliography| Notes| Illustrations| References| About the author Abstracts English Français Fears of coal shortage emerged in Great Britain at the end of the 18th century, when coal became not only the basis of British people’s daily lives, but also the staple of their economy. At the beginning of the 1870s, these fears intensified, following the publication of Stanley Jevons’s book The Coal Question in 1865 and a very long and difficult coal famine.
